summary:
The Spread of Rice Farming to Japan (c. 300–100 BC) was the introduction of wet-rice agriculture from continental Asia (via Korea) to the Japanese islands, which transformed Japanese society from the hunter-gatherer and forager lifestyle of the Jomon period to the agricultural base that supported the [[event:1169|Yayoi Period Japan]]'s growing population and social complexity.

Rice cultivation, originating in the Yangtze River delta of China, had spread to Korea by around 1000 BC. During the [[event:1169|Yayoi Period]] migrants from the Korean peninsula brought wet-rice agriculture techniques to northern Kyushu, from where it spread eastward through Honshu over the following centuries. The transition from foraging to farming was not immediate or universal — Jomon populations in northeastern Japan and Hokkaido maintained their traditional lifestyle much longer. Where rice agriculture was adopted, it transformed: settlement patterns (villages near irrigable fields), social organization (hierarchies based on land control and surplus storage), material culture (pottery, metal tools), and religious practices (rice-related rituals that persist in Shinto to this day). The paddy field landscape of rural Japan, so central to Japanese aesthetics and identity, has its roots in this period.
